intro: The pivotal question between Aider and Iterative.ai is how much of your setup stays portable: Aider carries high lock-in and Iterative.ai carries low lock-in. Aider is terminal AI pair programmer that edits files via Git commits. Iterative.ai is open-source ML ops — DVC and CML for data/model versioning in Git workflows. Pick Aider when the job is apps and you accept high lock-in; pick Iterative.ai when it is engineering-heavy ml teams that want git-based data and experiment workflows and you accept low lock-in. The data we have shows Aider at mostly transparent pricing and docs with solid developer experience, and Iterative.ai at mostly transparent pricing and docs with a polished developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Aider is a poor fit for simple projects, and Iterative.ai is a poor fit for data science teams preferring notebook-first uis over git semantics. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list.
quick_take: Aider is for terminal AI pair programmer that edits files; Iterative.ai is for open-source ML ops — DVC and CML; decide based on how portable the setup must stay.
verdict_a: Choose Aider if deep integration is an acceptable price for the depth it buys and the work maps to terminal AI pair programmer that edits files via Git commits.
verdict_b: Choose Iterative.ai if day-to-day developer ergonomics are a priority and the work lines up with open-source ML ops — DVC and CML for data/model versioning in Git workflows.

faq.a: Neither Aider nor Iterative.ai publishes entry pricing that lets us compare directly — Aider uses a freemium model and Iterative.ai uses a freemium model. Get quotes from both before assuming one is cheaper.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Aider to Iterative.ai?
faq.a: Leaving Aider for Iterative.ai is the harder direction: Aider is rated high lock-in, so exporting data and rebuilding integrations is the main cost. Going the other way (Iterative.ai to Aider) is usually easier.

Iterative.ai scores higher on developer experience in our data (5/5 vs 4/5). The gap usually shows up in setup friction and daily workflow speed. Confirm it on your own stack before treating the rating as final.

faq.q: Is Iterative.ai a good alternative to Aider?
faq.a: Yes — Iterative.ai is a reasonable alternative to Aider for apps. The practical differences are freemium-vs-freemium billing and high-vs-low lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Iterative.ai as a credible swap.
